record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Town clerks issue marriage licenses; county probate courts also involved. State registration began 1901 but towns recorded vital events much earlier.
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1901 (state registration). Town clerks recorded births, marriages, and deaths from the 1600s; published in 'New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901.'

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — New Hampshire Record Losses; NH DARM guides; New Hampshire has no burned counties — record survival is generally excellent across all 10 counties.

burned_counties_summary.notes: New Hampshire has no burned counties. Record survival is excellent statewide, benefiting from the New England town clerk tradition of continuous record-keeping.

administrative_notes: New Hampshire was settled in 1623 (Dover, Portsmouth), became a royal province in 1679 (separating from Massachusetts), and ratified the Constitution as the ninth state on June 21, 1788. Originally divided into five counties in 1769 (Rockingham, Strafford, Hillsborough, Cheshire, Grafton). The remaining five counties were formed by 1840. NH uses a New England town government system — vital records (births, marriages, deaths) were recorded by town clerks, not county officials. Land records are at the county Register of Deeds level. Probate is at the county level. The NH Division of Archives and Records Management (DARM) in Concord is the central state repository for provincial, state, and transferred county records.

core_repositories.source_name: New Hampshire Division of Archives and Records Management (DARM)
core_repositories.description: Central state repository under the Secretary of State. Holds provincial records (1679-1788), state government records, transferred county court records, vital records, military records, and the New Hampshire State Papers. Essential first stop for any NH genealogical research.

core_repositories.source_name: New Hampshire Historical Society (NHHS) — Tuck Library
core_repositories.description: Premier genealogical research library in NH. Tuck Library holds published genealogies, town histories, manuscript collections, church records, and family papers. Extensive vertical files organized by family name and town. Collections include Canterbury and Enfield Shaker records, Scotch-Irish settlement documents, and White Mountains tourism papers.
State library with genealogy collection including NH town histories, county histories, published vital records, and census microfilm. Interlibrary loan access to genealogical materials statewide.

core_repositories.source_name: Dartmouth College Rauner Special Collections Library
core_repositories.description: Major manuscript repository for northern NH and the Upper Connecticut River Valley. Holdings include family papers, town records, business records, White Mountains tourism materials, and Daniel Webster papers. Finding aids online.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including town vital records, probate records, deed indexes, church records, and cemetery transcriptions. Extensive microfilm holdings from partnership with DARM and county offices.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch — New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901
familysearch_collections.description: Major digitized collection of town-level birth, marriage, and death records from across NH, compiled and published as part of the statewide vital records project. Covers records from the 1600s through 1901.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / NH State Library
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic NH newspapers including the New Hampshire Sentinel (Keene, from 1799), Concord newspapers, Manchester Union, and smaller town papers. Coverage from late 1700s through mid-1900s.
Subscription database with additional digitized NH newspapers not in Chronicling America, including obituaries and social notices.

military_records.source_name: NH Adjutant General Reports — Revolutionary War through Civil War
military_records.description: Published reports of the NH Adjutant General listing soldiers, their units, and service records. Covers Revolutionary War muster rolls, War of 1812, and Civil War regimental histories. Digitized and available online.

military_records.source_name: NARA — New Hampshire Military Service Records
military_records.description: Federal military service records, pension files, and bounty land warrants for NH soldiers. Includes Rangers and militia from colonial frontier wars (1689-1763), Revolutionary War, and later conflicts.

military_records.source_name: NH Provincial and Frontier War Records
military_records.description: Records of NH's participation in King William's War, Queen Anne's War, Lovewell's War, and the French and Indian War. Includes scalp bounties, garrison duty rolls, and captivity accounts. Published in NH State Papers series.

land_and_property.description: Centralized online portal providing access to land records from all 10 NH county Register of Deeds offices. Grantor/grantee indexes and scanned deed images. Coverage varies by county but all 10 counties are represented.

land_and_property.source_name: Masonian Proprietors Records and NH Land Grants
land_and_property.description: Original township grants from the Masonian Proprietors (heirs of Capt. John Mason's 1629 grant), provincial governor charters (Wentworth grants), and state grants. Published in NH State Papers and NH Provincial Papers. Essential for understanding original land ownership patterns.

vital_records.source_name: NH Division of Vital Records Administration
vital_records.description: State-level vital records (birth, death, marriage, divorce) from 1901 to present. Older records (pre-1901) at town clerk offices or compiled in 'New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901.'

vital_records.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 (Published Series)
vital_records.description: Compiled and published town-by-town vital records covering births, marriages, and deaths from the earliest settlement (1600s) through 1901. A foundational resource for NH genealogy. Available on FamilySearch and in print at NHHS Tuck Library.

Town clerks continue to maintain local vital records. For current records and certified copies, contact the town clerk where the event occurred. New Hampshire has 234 towns, 13 cities, and other unincorporated places.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Diocese of Manchester Archives — Catholic Records
church_and_cemetery.description: Sacramental registers (baptism, marriage, burial) from Catholic parishes across NH. Critical for French Canadian, Irish, and other immigrant communities especially in Manchester, Nashua, Berlin, Dover, and Claremont. Diocese covers all of NH.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Rhode Island Historical Society / Special Collections
church_and_cemetery.description: Records of NH Quaker meetings including Dover, Hampton Falls, and Weare. Births, marriages, deaths, removals, and meeting minutes from the 1600s through 1800s.

Volunteer organization that has transcribed and published inscriptions from hundreds of NH cemeteries. Publications available at NHHS Tuck Library and NH State Library. FindAGrave and BillionGraves complement these efforts.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society with a research library in Stratham. Publishes 'The New Hampshire Genealogical Record' quarterly. Maintains surname files, Bible records, and town files. Library focused on southeastern NH families.
Comprehensive research guide for NH genealogy including county-by-county record inventories, repository guides, and research strategies. Free access.

Specialized society for French Canadian genealogy in New England. Library in Manchester with extensive Quebec parish register transcriptions, Drouin collection access, and Franco-American family files. Essential for tracing mill-town immigrant families.

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: Historical County Information — No Extinct Counties
historical_counties_extinct.description: New Hampshire has had no extinct or renamed counties. All 10 counties created between 1769 and 1840 remain active today. The five original counties (1769) were Rockingham, Strafford, Hillsborough, Cheshire, and Grafton. Coos was formed in 1803, Merrimack in 1823, Sullivan in 1827, and Belknap and Carroll in 1840.
historical_counties_extinct.notes: Pre-1769: The Province of New Hampshire had no county divisions. Records from before 1769 are in provincial records at DARM. Pre-1679: NH was part of Massachusetts Bay Colony; some records may be in Massachusetts state archives.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA New England Region, Waltham MA
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Federal court naturalization records for NH. Includes declarations of intention and petitions for naturalization from US District and Circuit Courts in NH. Critical for French Canadian, Irish, Greek, and Eastern European immigrants in mill towns.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: American-Canadian Genealogical Society — Quebec Parish Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Transcriptions and indexes of Quebec Catholic parish registers used to trace French Canadian families who migrated to NH mill towns (Manchester, Nashua, Berlin, Dover, Claremont). Drouin Collection microfilm and PRDH database access.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: NH DARM — County Court Naturalization Records
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from NH county courts (Superior Court and earlier courts). Declarations of intention and petitions transferred to DARM from county courthouses. Coverage varies by county.
